content_markdown:
A satellite of mass $1500\text{ kg}$ is in the Earth's gravitational field. It moves from a point where the gravitational potential is $-30\text{ MJ kg}^{-1}$ to a point where the gravitational potential is $-20\text{ MJ kg}^{-1}$.

What is the direction of movement of the satellite and the change in its gravitational potential energy?

diagram_metadata.description: A table showing four options (A-D) with two columns: 'Direction of movement of satellite' and 'Change in gravitational potential energy / GJ'.
labels_to_preserve: Change in gravitational potential energy / GJ

options.label: A
options.text: Direction: away from Earth; Change in GPE / GJ: 15

options.label: B
options.text: Direction: away from Earth; Change in GPE / GJ: 75

options.label: C
options.text: Direction: towards Earth; Change in GPE / GJ: 15

options.label: D
options.text: Direction: towards Earth; Change in GPE / GJ: 75

vibe_explanation: First, determine the direction: gravitational potential increases (becomes less negative) as you move further away from the Earth, so moving from $-30\text{ MJ kg}^{-1}$ to $-20\text{ MJ kg}^{-1}$ is moving away. Second, calculate the change in energy: $\Delta E_p = m \Delta V = 1500\text{ kg} \times (-20 - (-30))\text{ MJ kg}^{-1} = 1500 \times 10\text{ MJ} = 15000\text{ MJ}$. Since $1\text{ GJ} = 1000\text{ MJ}$, the change is $15\text{ GJ}$.
